body:
# Comprehensive Documentation Site Overhaul

## 🎯 Objective
Transform OmniRoute's scattered documentation into a comprehensive, structured documentation site with interactive features, advanced search, and professional organization inspired by industry best practices.

## 📋 Problem Statement
Current documentation system limitations:
- **Scattered Organization**: Markdown files spread across repository with limited navigation
- **No Dedicated Site**: Lack of professional documentation portal
- **Limited Discoverability**: Poor search and feature discovery
- **Static Content**: No interactive elements or API testing
- **Inconsistent Quality**: Varied formatting and structure

## ✨ Proposed Solution

### New Documentation Structure


### Key Features
- **Interactive API Documentation**: Swagger/OpenAPI with try-it-now functionality
- **Advanced Search**: Algolia/DocSearch with autocomplete and filtering
- **Versioned Content**: Clear version tags and migration guides
- **Code Examples**: Runnable examples with copy-to-clipboard
- **Visual Aids**: Interactive Mermaid diagrams and architecture visualizations
- **Responsive Design**: Mobile-friendly with dark mode support
- **Analytics**: Usage tracking and feedback system

### Technology Stack
- **Framework**: Next.js (consistent with OmniRoute dashboard)
- **Content**: MDX (Markdown + React components)
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS (matches OmniRoute UI)
- **Search**: Algolia DocSearch or Fuse.js
- **Deployment**: Vercel with preview deployments

## 📈 Expected Benefits
- **50% increase** in documentation page views
- **30% reduction** in support requests for basic questions
- **40% increase** in time spent on documentation pages
- **90% positive** feedback on documentation quality
- **25% increase** in community contributions

## 🔧 Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Foundation (2 weeks)
- Set up Next.js documentation framework
- Design component library with OmniRoute theme
- Implement MDX processing pipeline
- Set up CI/CD pipeline with preview deployments

### Phase 2: Content Migration (4 weeks)
- Audit and categorize existing documentation
- Create automated migration tools
- Migrate core content with quality validation
- Implement redirects from old URLs

### Phase 3: Advanced Features (3 weeks)
- Implement search functionality with Algolia
- Add interactive API documentation
- Create versioning system
- Implement analytics and feedback

### Phase 4: Testing & Launch (2 weeks)
- Comprehensive testing (unit, integration, E2E)
- Performance optimization
- Accessibility validation
- Final review and official launch

## 📊 Success Metrics
- All content successfully migrated and validated
- Search functionality achieves ≥90% relevance in test queries
- Accessibility compliance (WCAG 2.1 AA) verified
- Performance metrics meet targets (Lighthouse ≥90)
- 50% increase in documentation engagement

## 🎯 Definition of Done
- Complete content migration with validation
- All interactive features fully functional
- Search system operational with ≥90% relevance
- Accessibility compliance verified
- Performance targets achieved
- Analytics and feedback system implemented
- Comprehensive documentation completed

## 📝 Additional Context
This overhaul will create a professional documentation portal that improves user onboarding, feature discovery, and overall satisfaction while maintaining consistency with OmniRoute's brand and technical stack. The new site will serve as the primary resource for users, developers, and contributors.
title: feat: Comprehensive Documentation Site Overhaul

body:
# Manifest Routing Integration - Tier Resolution & Specificity Detection

## 🎯 Objective
Enhance OmniRoute's combo routing system by integrating Manifest's sophisticated tier resolution and specificity detection logic to create a more intelligent, context-aware routing engine that optimizes for both performance and cost.

## 📋 Problem Statement
The current combo routing system, while functional with 13 strategies, lacks:
- **Tier Resolution**: Provider classification based on performance, cost, and capabilities
- **Specificity Detection**: Content complexity analysis for optimal route selection
- **Adaptive Strategies**: Dynamic adjustment based on real-time metrics and query requirements

## ✨ Proposed Solution

### Core Components
1. **Tier Resolution System**
   - 3-level classification: static rules → heuristics → specificity-based
   - Dynamic tier assignment based on real-time provider metrics
   - Integration with existing provider health monitoring

2. **Specificity Detection**
   - Query complexity scoring (0-100 scale)
   - Content-type analysis for optimal provider matching
   - Historical performance data integration

3. **Adaptive Combo Strategies**
   - New auto-optimized strategy with dynamic adjustment
   - Tier-aware fallback logic
   - Cost-performance balancing algorithms

### Integration Points
- **PR #1918 Auto-Assessment**: Leverage existing health monitoring and performance data
- **Existing Combo System**: Maintain backward compatibility with current strategies
- **Metrics Pipeline**: Feed routing decisions into analytics for continuous improvement

## 📈 Expected Benefits
- **15-25%** improvement in routing success rates for complex queries
- **10-20%** reduction in API costs through optimal provider utilization
- **30% faster** routing decisions via optimized tier selection
- **Enhanced reliability** with smarter, context-aware fallback logic

## 🔧 Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Foundation (2-3 weeks)
- Research Manifest's tier resolution algorithms
- Design system architecture with integration points
- Implement basic tier classification framework
- Set up test infrastructure and benchmarks

### Phase 2: Core Implementation (4-5 weeks)
- Implement 3-tier resolution system (static/heuristic/specificity)
- Develop specificity scoring algorithm
- Create adaptive combo strategies
- Integrate with PR #1918 auto-assessment system

### Phase 3: Optimization & Testing (3 weeks)
- Performance tuning and benchmarking
- Comprehensive test suite (unit, integration, e2e)
- Documentation and examples
- Gradual rollout and monitoring

## 📊 Success Metrics
- Routing success rate improvement (A/B testing)
- Cost reduction verified through usage analytics
- Performance benchmarks showing latency improvements
- User satisfaction surveys on routing reliability

## 🔍 Related Work
- PR #1918: Auto-Assessment and Self-Healing Combo Engine
- Manifest routing engine analysis
- Current combo strategies documentation

## 🎯 Definition of Done
- Tier resolution achieves ≥95% accuracy in classification
- Specificity detection shows ≥90% correlation with manual scoring
- Adaptive strategies demonstrate ≥15% improvement in benchmarks
- Integration with auto-assessment system verified
- All performance benchmarks meet targets
- Comprehensive documentation completed

## 📝 Additional Context
This enhancement builds upon PR #1918's auto-assessment capabilities, adding intelligent tier-based routing and query-specific optimization to create a sophisticated, cost-effective routing system that maintains OmniRoute's commitment to performance and reliability.
title: feat: Manifest Routing Integration - Tier Resolution & Specificity Detection

body:
### Problem / Use Case

OmniRoute currently tries all accounts from lets say Codex provider, even though some of them have no more usage available.

### Proposed Solution

Make the already existing buttons "track 5-hour quota" and "track weekly quota" on `/dashboard/providers/codex` page would make OmniRoute check the quota of the account before attempting to send a query and skip it if on 0%. This could work for other providers like Claude Code in the same way.

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Acceptance Criteria

Have accounts without any usage left skipped automatically when quota tracking is turned on.

### Area

Proxy / Routing

### Related Provider(s)

Codex, Claude Code...

### Additional Context

_No response_

### Expected Test Plan

_No response_
title: [Feature] Skip (Codex) accounts that have the weekly or 5h quota exhausted

body:
### Problem / Use Case

OmniRoute supports a very large provider ecosystem, but Devin AI is not currently available as a built-in provider.

This matters because Devin is a strong coding-focused agent for longer-running engineering tasks, and OmniRoute already positions itself as a universal gateway for coding agents and model providers. Right now, users who want to evaluate or route work to Devin cannot do that from the same OmniRoute endpoint or include Devin in combos/fallback chains.

## Why the current custom-provider path is not enough

As far as I understand, OmniRoute's generic custom provider support is for OpenAI-compatible and Anthropic-compatible endpoints.

Devin does expose an official API, but it is session-based and organization-scoped rather than a drop-in `/chat/completions`-compatible endpoint. Because of that, Devin likely needs a first-class provider adapter instead of a generic custom endpoint configuration.

## Use cases

- Route repo-level coding, debugging, and refactor tasks to Devin from the same OmniRoute setup used for Codex, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, etc.
- Use Devin as a higher-latency but higher-capability fallback for complex implementation tasks.
- Compare Devin against other coding-oriented providers inside the same OmniRoute combo/routing policy.
- Evaluate Devin inside OmniRoute without reconfiguring clients every time.

### Proposed Solution

## Proposed solution

Add Devin as a built-in provider with a custom executor.

High-level implementation sketch:

1. Register a new `devin` provider in `src/shared/constants/providers.ts`.
2. Add Devin provider/model metadata in `open-sse/config/providerRegistry.ts`.
3. Implement a custom executor in `open-sse/executors/` that:
   - authenticates with Devin service-user credentials
   - targets the organization API under `https://api.devin.ai/v3/organizations/{org_id}`
   - creates a new Devin session for a fresh OmniRoute request
   - sends follow-up messages to an existing Devin session when conversation continuity is available
   - polls session state and/or session messages until a stable assistant result is available
   - maps the final Devin output back into OmniRoute's OpenAI-compatible response shape
4. Add dashboard/provider settings for:
   - `DEVIN_API_KEY` (service-user credential)
   - `DEVIN_ORG_ID`
   - optional defaults such as session tags, repo selection, or ACU limits
5. Make the provider eligible for direct selection and combo routing.

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Acceptance Criteria

## Acceptance criteria

- Devin appears in the Providers UI as a connectable provider.
- A user can store Devin credentials and validate the connection.
- OmniRoute can send a basic text request to Devin and return a usable assistant response through `/v1/chat/completions` and/or `/v1/responses`.
- Follow-up prompts can either reuse a Devin session or the limitation is explicitly documented for MVP behavior.
- Devin can be targeted directly in combos.
- Common Devin session states/errors (for example approval-needed, quota/usage exhaustion, or suspended sessions) are surfaced as clear OmniRoute errors.
- Documentation explains setup, limitations, and expected latency/behavior differences versus normal token-streaming LLM providers.

## Suggested MVP scope

MVP can be text-only and non-streaming/polling-based if needed.

Not required for the first version:
- embeddings
- image/audio endpoints
- tool-calling parity
- full real-time streaming parity with standard LLM providers

### Area

Provider Support

### Related Provider(s)

_No response_

### Additional Context

## Additional context

This would also be useful for users who want to evaluate Devin during onboarding or trial access without changing their entire client setup.

### Expected Test Plan

_No response_
title: [Feature] devin.ai
